annotations.label: Mean

anchor.snippet: A mean is a quantity representing

annotations.note: There is no general formalization of the abstract notion of "mean", but MeasureTheory.average (⨍ x, f x ∂μ) provides one concrete mean.

annotations.label: Arithmetic mean (lead)

anchor.snippet: is the sum of the values divided by the number of values

annotations.note: Finset.centroid is the affine combination with equal weights 1/n, which over ℝ is exactly (1/n)·∑ values, generalizing the arithmetic mean.

annotations.label: Pythagorean means

anchor.snippet: the three classical Pythagorean means are the arithmetic mean

annotations.note: The three means appear as expressions in the AM-GM/HM-GM inequality lemmas, but "Pythagorean means" as a grouped concept is not defined.

annotations.label: Arithmetic mean (AM)

anchor.snippet: is the sum of all of the numbers divided by their count

annotations.note: Finset.centroid (affine combination with weights 1/n) generalizes the arithmetic mean of a finite list of numbers.

annotations.label: Arithmetic mean example

anchor.snippet: For example, the arithmetic mean of five values

annotations.note: A concrete numeric example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Geometric mean (GM)

anchor.snippet: The geometric mean is an average that is useful for sets of positive numbers

annotations.note: The geometric mean appears as the expression (∏ z i ^ w i)^(1/∑w) inside the AM-GM lemmas, but there is no standalone geometric-mean definition.

annotations.label: Geometric mean example

anchor.snippet: For example, the geometric mean of five values

annotations.note: A concrete numeric example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Harmonic mean (HM)

anchor.snippet: The harmonic mean is an average which is useful for sets of numbers

annotations.note: The harmonic mean appears as the expression (∑w)/(∑ w/z) inside the HM-GM lemma, but there is no standalone harmonic-mean definition.

annotations.label: Harmonic mean example

anchor.snippet: For example, the harmonic mean of the five values

annotations.note: A concrete numeric example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Five pumps example

anchor.snippet: If we have five pumps that can empty a tank

annotations.note: A word-problem application is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: AM-GM-HM inequality

anchor.section: Relationship between AM, GM, and HM
anchor.snippet: AM, GM, and HM of nonnegative real numbers satisfy these inequalities

annotations.note: GM ≤ AM is Real.geom_mean_le_arith_mean and HM ≤ GM is Real.harm_mean_le_geom_mean, together giving the chain in weighted/general form.

annotations.label: Mean of observations

anchor.snippet: The mean of a set of observations is the arithmetic average of the values

annotations.note: The sample mean of observations is the equal-weight arithmetic average, formalized as Finset.centroid over ℝ.

annotations.label: Mean of a probability distribution

anchor.snippet: The mean of a probability distribution is the long-run arithmetic average value

annotations.note: The mean (expectation) is the integral ∫ x ∂μ, equal to MeasureTheory.average of the identity for a probability measure, but no dedicated "distribution mean" definition exists.

annotations.label: Mean of discrete distribution

anchor.snippet: For a discrete probability distribution

annotations.note: A discrete-distribution mean ∑ x·p(x) is a special case of the integral/expectation; only the general average/integral infrastructure exists.

annotations.label: Mean of continuous distribution

anchor.snippet: For a continuous distribution

annotations.note: A continuous-distribution mean ∫ x·f(x) dx is a special case of the integral/expectation captured by the general average/integral infrastructure.

annotations.label: Power mean

anchor.snippet: abstracts several other means

annotations.note: No generalized (power/Hölder) mean definition exists; only related Lp/rpow inequalities are present, not the mean itself.

annotations.label: Quasi-arithmetic mean

anchor.snippet: also known as the quasi-arithmetic mean

annotations.note: The quasi-arithmetic (Kolmogorov) mean f⁻¹((1/n)∑f(xᵢ)) is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weighted arithmetic mean

anchor.snippet: is used if one wants to combine average values from different sized samples

annotations.note: Finset.affineCombination ∑ wᵢ·pᵢ with weights summing to 1 is the weighted arithmetic mean, generalized to affine spaces.

annotations.label: Truncated mean

anchor.snippet: one can use a truncated mean

annotations.note: The truncated/trimmed mean is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Interquartile mean

anchor.snippet: A specific example of a truncated mean is the interquartile mean

annotations.note: The interquartile mean is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mean of a function

anchor.snippet: This can happen when calculating the mean value

annotations.note: MeasureTheory.average (⨍ x, f x ∂μ = (μ univ)⁻¹·∫f) generalizes the mean value (1/(b−a))∫ₐᵇ f of a function.

annotations.label: Circular mean

anchor.section: Mean of angles and cyclical quantities
anchor.snippet: These quantities can be averaged using the circular mean

annotations.note: The circular mean of angles (atan2 of summed sines/cosines) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fréchet mean

anchor.snippet: The Fréchet mean gives a manner for determining

annotations.note: The Fréchet mean (minimizer of summed squared metric distances) is not defined in Mathlib; "Fréchet" occurrences refer to derivatives.

annotations.label: Center of a triangle

anchor.snippet: thousands of different definitions for the center of a triangle

annotations.note: Specific triangle/simplex centers (circumcenter, incenter, centroid) are formalized, but there is no general "triangle center" abstraction.

annotations.label: Swanson's rule

anchor.snippet: This is an approximation to the mean for a moderately skewed distribution

annotations.note: Swanson's rule (0.3·P10 + 0.4·P50 + 0.3·P90) is not formalized in Mathlib.
